title: Train your focus
description: Attention is trainable — beat distraction and find your flow.
summaryPoints: Single-task: do one thing at a time, because every switch costs you time and errors
Kill the cues — phone in another room, notifications off, tabs closed, one study spot
Run Pomodoros (25 min focus, 5 min break) to protect attention and make starting easy
Find flow with a clear goal, a matched challenge, and zero interruptions
Use a 10-breath reset to notice you've drifted and gently come back

content.title: Your attention is a battery
content.body:
Picture your attention as a phone battery you can't recharge mid-day. Every notification, open tab, and 'quick check' drains it.

Here's the good news: focus isn't a personality trait you either have or don't. It's a **muscle**. The more you train it, the longer you can hold it — and the easier hard work starts to feel.
content.imageAlt: A bullseye with a single arrow hitting the center

content.title: Multitasking is a lie
content.body:
You don't actually do two things at once. Your brain rapidly **switches** between them — and every switch has a cost: lost time and more mistakes (it's called switch cost).

Doom-scrolling makes it worse. Constant little hits of new content train your brain to crave distraction. The fix is simple to say, hard to do: **single-task**. One thing at a time.

content.prompt: Multitasking is an efficient way to study.
content.explanation: What feels like multitasking is really fast task-switching, and every switch wastes time and adds errors.

content.prompt: What's the real cost of 'multitasking' between homework and your group chat?
options: Each switch wastes time and adds errors
Nothing — your brain handles both at once
content.explanation: Your brain switches instead of splitting, and that switch cost piles up across a study session.

content.title: Kill the cues
content.body:
Willpower loses to a buzzing phone. So remove the temptation before it starts:
- **Phone in another room** — even face-down on your desk, its mere presence drains focus
- **Close extra tabs** and turn off notifications
- **Use one study spot** every time, so your brain learns 'here = focus'

You're not weak for getting distracted. You're just fighting cues you don't have to.

content.prompt: Where should your phone be while you study?
content.context: You've got two hours of focused work ahead.
options: Screen-up so you can see who texts
content.explanation: Even silent and face-down, a phone's mere presence drains focus — distance beats willpower.

items.text: Replying to the group chat mid-task

content.instructions: One word is wrong. Tap it.
content.sentence: To get more done, you should multitask as much as possible.
content.explanation: Multitasking is really task-switching, and every switch costs time and focus. One thing at a time wins.

content.title: The Pomodoro trick
content.body:
Starting is the hard part. The **Pomodoro** technique shrinks it: work in one **25-minute** focus block, then take a **5-minute** break. After four blocks, take a longer break.

Why it works: 25 minutes feels doable, the timer guards your attention, and the breaks keep you from burning out. 'Just one Pomodoro' is way easier to start than 'study for three hours.'

content.prompt: One Pomodoro focus block is about ___ minutes long.
content.explanation: A classic Pomodoro is 25 minutes of focus followed by a 5-minute break — short enough to start, long enough to get real work done.

content.prompt: Put these in order to set up one focused study session.
steps: Put your phone in another room
Sit down at your one study spot
Set a 25-minute timer and pick one task
Work until the timer rings, then take a 5-minute break
content.explanation: Remove the cue, settle into your focus spot, start a single timed task, then rest — that's one clean Pomodoro.

content.title: Flow and the wandering mind
content.body:
**Flow** is that state where time disappears and the work just clicks. It shows up when a **clear goal** meets a **matched challenge** — not too easy, not too hard — with **no interruptions**.

But minds wander. That's normal. **Mindfulness** is paying attention to the present on purpose, without judging it. It trains the exact muscle that notices you've drifted and gently brings you back to the page.

content.prompt: Match each technique to why it works.

pairs.right: Removes the cue that drains focus

content.prompt: What does practising mindfulness actually train?
options: Noticing your mind has wandered and gently coming back
Emptying your mind of every thought forever
Studying for longer without any breaks
Feeling guilty whenever you get distracted
content.explanation: Mindfulness builds the 'notice you've drifted and return' muscle — the same one that pulls you back to studying.

content.title: The 10-breath reset
content.body:
Here's a reset you can do anywhere, even mid-study-meltdown: take **10 slow breaths** and just notice the breath going in and out.

Your mind will wander — that's fine. Each time it does, gently bring it back. **The returning is the rep.** A minute of this lowers stress and sharpens concentration, so you slide back into work calmer than you left it.

content.prompt: If your mind wanders during a breathing reset, you've failed and should give up.
content.explanation: Wandering is expected — noticing it and gently returning is the whole exercise. The return is the rep.

content.prompt: Design your own focus ritual: where will you study, what will you do with your phone, and which one mindfulness reset will you try?
content.context: Pick things you'll actually do tonight — a ritual only works if it's realistic.
content.placeholder: Tonight I'll study at… my phone will be… and I'll reset with…
content.explanation: A ritual you repeat in one spot trains your brain that 'here = focus' — and a built-in reset means a wandering mind never derails the whole session.
